risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "An allocation list was prepared using computer-generated random numbers and a block size of six. The manufacturer prepared numbered packs of tablets corresponding to the allocation list. Assignment to the three treatment arms was done separately for three cohorts of children stratified by HIV status of child and mother..." Comment: The study explicitly states that the allocation sequence was generated using computer-generated random numbers, with block randomization and stratification. This is a clearly random method of sequence generation.

Quote: "An allocation list was prepared using computer-generated random numbers and a block size of six. The manufacturer prepared numbered packs of tablets corresponding to the allocation list... Investigators, study staff and participants were blind to the treatment assignments." Also: "All supplements were packaged in identical blister packs of seven tablets. All trial supplement tablets had the same color, form, texture and taste." Comment: The allocation list was implemented through manufacturer-prepared numbered treatment packs, with identical appearance and blinding of investigators/staff. This is consistent with a prepackaged, numbered container system that would prevent the person enrolling participants from foreseeing the upcoming assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Investigators, study staff and participants were blind to the treatment assignments." and "All supplements were packaged in identical blister packs of seven tablets. All trial supplement tablets had the same color, form, texture and taste." Comment: The report explicitly states that participants and study personnel were blinded, and describes matching appearance/taste/packaging of supplements, making successful blinding plausible and unlikely to be broken. Because key outcomes include diarrhoea and growth, which could be influenced if blinding failed, this explicit and credible blinding supports a Low risk judgement.

Quote: "Investigators, study staff and participants were blind to the treatment assignments." and "Length, head circumference, mid-upper arm circumference and triceps skin-fold thickness were recorded by a trained study nurse... Hemoglobin determination was done at the clinic by study nurses trained in the standardized use of a portable HemoCue system... Information on diarrhoea was collected during weekly home visits[17]." Comment: Outcome assessors were study staff/nurses/field workers, and the paper explicitly states that investigators and study staff were blinded to treatment assignment. Reported outcomes in this report are mainly objective or standardized measurements (anthropometry, hemoglobin/anemia) and diarrhoea ascertainment in a trial where participants were also blinded, making detection bias unlikely. No unblinded subjective outcome assessor is identified.

Quote: "Information on length Z-scores at two or more occasions was available for 317 (85%) of 373 children enrolled in the study (Figure 1)... Baseline hemoglobin measurements were available for 254 (68%) of the 373 children, baseline and 12 month hemoglobin measurements were available for 210 children (Figure 1)." and "There was no significant difference between treatment arms in the proportion of children who were lost to follow-up (including withdrawal or death)." Comment: The paper reports some overall post-randomization missingness, but for the review outcomes of interest (death, diarrhoea, lower respiratory tract infection, malaria, vomiting) it does not provide per-arm randomized versus analysed numbers at the relevant analysed time point, nor raw per-arm attrition counts/reasons that would allow calculation of differential loss. A statement of "no significant difference" without the underlying numbers is insufficient to confirm low risk, and absence of these data is not positive evidence for high risk. Therefore attrition bias for the outcomes of interest is unclear.
